coredata.dc:title: A strategy of enhancing photoactivity of TiO2 via facet-dependent pyrolysis of dicyandiamide
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Tao
Yin, Wenchao
Yu, Xuelian
An, Xiaoqiang
coredata.dc:description: A deep understanding of the relationship between surface chemistry and activity enhancement of photocatalysts at the molecular level is still lacking despite the increasing interest in this exciting field. In this paper, the influence of exposed facets on the pyrolysis of organic modifiers and thereafter the photoactivity of photocatalysts was investigated by using the thermal pyrolysis of dicyanodiamide (DCDA) over TiO2 as an example. Experimental results revealed that {001}-faceted TiO2 was favorable for the formation of carbon dots modified TiO2 nanosheets. In contrast, the deep pyrolysis of organic precursor over {101} facets contributed to the formation of oxygen-deficient TiO2. Benefitted from the oxygen vacancy mediated mechanism, nearly order-of-magnitude improvement in hydrogen evolution rate was achieved for 101-faceted TiO2 treated by DCDA, which was much higher than the treated 001-TiO2 and P25. This work not only provides new insights into the facet-dependent activity of TiO2 photocatalysts, but also offers a facile strategy for exploring high-efficiency photocatalysts for water splitting through facet tailoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanium dioxide
Crystal facet
Thermal pyrolysis
Oxygen vacancy
Hydrogen evolution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic synthetic routes for faceted T001/CDs and Ov-T101.
TEM and HR-TEM images of T001 (a,b) and T101 (c,d).
HRTEM images of T001-DCDA (a) and T101-DCDA (b).
Raman spectra of T001, T001-DCDA (a) and T101, T101-DCDA (b). The insets are the enlarged parts from 1000 to 2000 cm−1.
(a) ESR spectra of T001-DCDA and T101-DCDA, (b) XPS-VB spectra of T101 and T101-DCDA, (c) Ti 2p XPS spectra of T001 and T001-DCDA, (d) Ti 2p XPS spectra of T101 and T101-DCDA.

(a) Hydrogen production rates of T001 with different amount of DCDA, (b) Hydrogen production rates of T101 with different amount of DCDA (c) Comparisons of the hydrogen production rates of different catalytic systems. (d) Cycling measurements of hydrogen generation through photocatalytic water splitting with T101-30% DCDA.
Time-resolved PL decay curves of T001 and T001-DCDA emission at (a) 390 nm and (b) 475 nm, Time-resolved PL decay curves of T101 and T101-DCDA emission at (c) 390 nm and (d) 475 nm.
(a) Photovoltaic response of T101 and T101-DCDA, (b) Mott-Schottky plots of T101 and T101-DCDA, (c) Nyquist spectra of T101 and T101-DCDA, (d) Transient photocurrent response of T101 and T101-DCDA.

head.ce:title: A strategy of enhancing photoactivity of TiO2 via facet-dependent pyrolysis of dicyandiamide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Materials Utilization of Nonmetallic Minerals and Solid Wastes, National Laboratory of Mineral Materials, School of Materials Science and Technology, China University of Geosciences, 100083, Beijing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Materials Utilization of Nonmetallic Minerals and Solid Wastes
School of Materials Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory of Materials Utilization of Nonmetallic Minerals and Solid Wastes, National Laboratory of Mineral Materials, School of Materials Science and Technology, China University of Geosciences, 100083, Beijing, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Water and Ecology, State Key Joint Laboratory of Environment Simulation and Pollution Control, School of Environment, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Joint Laboratory of Environment Simulation and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Water and Ecology, State Key Joint Laboratory of Environment Simulation and Pollution Control, School of Environment, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A deep understanding of the relationship between surface chemistry and activity enhancement of photocatalysts at the molecular level is still lacking despite the increasing interest in this exciting field. In this paper, the influence of exposed facets on the pyrolysis of organic modifiers and thereafter the photoactivity of photocatalysts was investigated by using the thermal pyrolysis of dicyanodiamide (DCDA) over TiO2 as an example. Experimental results revealed that {001}-faceted TiO2 was favorable for the formation of carbon dots modified TiO2 nanosheets. In contrast, the deep pyrolysis of organic precursor over {101} facets contributed to the formation of oxygen-deficient TiO2. Benefitted from the oxygen vacancy mediated mechanism, nearly order-of-magnitude improvement in hydrogen evolution rate was achieved for 101-faceted TiO2 treated by DCDA, which was much higher than the treated 001-TiO2 and P25. This work not only provides new insights into the facet-dependent activity of TiO2 photocatalysts, but also offers a facile strategy for exploring high-efficiency photocatalysts for water splitting through facet tailoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Heterogeneous photocatalysis has been extensively investigated over the past decades and is considered as a critical technology to solve the energy crisis through solar energy harvesting and conversion. Due to the advantages of being low cost, non-toxic and long-term stable, TiO2 stands out as one of the most promising semiconductors for photocatalytic H2 production [1–3]. By far, impressive achievements have been achieved in exploring TiO2-based nanostructured photocatalysts. Unfortunately, catalytic efficiency of pristine TiO2 is seriously restricted by the inborn deficiency in visible light absorption and fast recombination of electrons and holes. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop high-performance TiO2-based photocatalysts for efficiently reducing water into hydrogen [4–8].

Several strategies, such as doping, surface acidification, defect modulation and construction of heterostructures, have been utilized to improve the photoactivity of TiO2 [9–12]. Among them, integrating TiO2 with carbonaceous materials has emerged as one of the most effective methods. As reported, carbon hybridization could not only endow the visible light response of TiO2, but also enhance the interfacial separation of charge carriers [13–16]. Either ex-situ or in-situ procedures have been employed to fabricate carbon-mediated TiO2 nanocatalysts. Especially, the in-situ pyrolysis of organic precursors is attracting tremendous attentions due to the facile and controllable processes. Using different types of hydrocarbon compounds as precursors, this in-situ strategy presents pronounced effect on constructing carbon-mediated photocatalysts with strongly coupled interface or uniformly distributed dopants [17–19].

Somewhat differently, our recent study demonstrated that pyrolysis of nitrogen-containing organic precursors under air atmosphere also exhibited significant impact on the electronic structure of TiO2 [20]. As a consequence of defect reconstruction, TiO2 with surface oxygen vacancies presented 12 times higher photocatalytic activity than pristine TiO2. In this facile and easy-to-operate method, the complicated interactions between reaction intermediates and TiO2 exhibited significant impact on the pyrolysis of organic compounds, as well as the formation and rearrangement of defects. With respect to TiO2-invovled pyrolysis, the decomposition of organic molecules is usually related to surface heterogeneous catalysis [21,22]. By far, detailed mechanisms involving these surface chemical processes have not been fundamentally understood. Especially, it has been reported that exposed facets of TiO2 governed the electronic structure, which could significantly affect the adsorption behavior and catalytic activity [23]. However, elucidating the correlation between facet-specific characteristics and thermal pyrolysis of carbon precursors still remains a challenging issue to exert the great potential of carbon-mediated photocatalysts for water splitting.

In the present study, we examined the use of pyrolysis strategy to enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2. We found that the thermal decomposition of dicyanodiamide (DCDA) was highly dependent with the exposed facets of TiO2. High-energy {001} facets of TiO2 catalyzed the carbonization of pyrolyzed intermediate molecules, resulting in the formation of carbon dots modified TiO2 nanosheets. However, weak interfacial interactions favored the deep pyrolysis of precursor molecules over {101}-faceted TiO2, generating oxygen-deficient TiO2 with significantly improved charge carrier separation. The role of oxygen vacancies as surface trap sites contributed to the order-of-magnitude improvement in hydrogen evolution rate of 101-faceted TiO2 treated by DCDA. Our work provides a facile strategy to construct high-performance TiO2-based nanocatalysts through facet-dependent pyrolysis.

To fabricate {001}-faceted TiO2, 1.5 mL of hydrofluoric acid and 12.5 mL of Titanium (IV) butoxide was added into a 50 mL Teflon autoclave. After stirring for 30 min, hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 200 °C for 24 h. The precipitants were collected by centrifugation and soaked in 0.1 M NaOH solution for 12 h. The white-colored TiO2 powders were obtained after repeated washing step, and finally annealed at 400 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace. The corresponding sample was denoted as T001.

A two-step hydrothermal reaction was used to fabricate {101}-faceted TiO2. Firstly, 1 g of P25 TiO2 nanoparticles was hydrothermally treated with 15 M KOH at 150 °C for 20 h. The precipitates were washed with DI water and acetic acid to pH = 5. Secondly, the obtained powders were dispersed into 15 mL of DI water and hydrothermally treated at 170 °C for 24 h. Finally, the white TiO2 nanoparticles were annealing at 400 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace to get T101.

To fabricate DCDA-treated samples, the obtained TiO2 and a certain amount of DCDA were suspended into 20 mL of DI water, respectively. The solution was stirred on a hot plate and dried overnight. The obtained powders were put into a crucible and heated to 400 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace. Through the facile heat-treatment, {001}- and {101}-faceted TiO2 treated by DCDA were obtained, which were denoted as T001-DCDA and T101-DCDA.

For comparison, P25-DCDA was fabricated through a similar procedure. Besides, to get oxygen-deficient T101, faceted TiO2 was placed in a quartz tube furnace equipped with a gas-flow-controlled system for hydrogen treatment. After heating at 450 °C for 4 h in an Ar/H2 flow, T101 with abundant oxygen vacancies (Vo-T101) were obtained.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was recorded on a Rigaku RINT 2100, at a voltage of 40 kV. The morphology of the samples was observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM-7600 F, JEOL) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL-2100 F). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was collected using the XPS spectrometers (ESCALab220I-XL). UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were obtained from a UV-vis-NIR spectrometer (Cary 5000). Electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis was measured by a Bruker E500 spectrometer. Raman spectra were obtained from a laser micro-Raman spectrometer (Horiba JobinYvon T64000). The photoluminescence spectra (PL) were obtained on a fluorometry (F-4500, Hitachi). The time-resolved fluorescence spectra were measured by a F900 fluorescence spectrometer.

Photocatalytic H2 evolution was tested in a gas-circulation system with a 300 W Xe lamp as the light source, the light intensity was calibrated to 100 mW/cm2. In a typical procedure, 15 mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed in 90 mL of distilled water and 10 mL of methanol as sacrificial reagent. H2PtCl6 was used to photodeposite 1 wt% of Pt as the co-catalyst. The hydrogen gas was analyzed by a chromatography (SP-6890, Shanghai Techcomp, nitrogen as a carrier gas).

Electrical impedance spectroscopic (EIS), transient photocurrent response and Mott-Schottky measurements were carried out using a three-electrode setup. Pt wire and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The electrolyte was 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution. EIS spectra were recorded over the frequency from 1 MHz to 100 mHz with the AC perturbation signal of 5 mV.

The crystal structure of different samples was studied by XRD. As shown in Figure S1, all the diffraction peaks in the patterns of T001 and T101 can be indexed to anatase titania with a tetragonal structure (JCPDS 75–1537). No obvious change of XRD patterns are observed for T001-DCDA and T101-DCDA. It indicates that the in-situ pyrolysis of DCDA presents ignorable influence on the phase structure of TiO2.

The morphology and exposed facets of TiO2 photocatalysts were observed by SEM and HRTEM. Figure S2 shows that both T001 and T101 are composed of nanoparticles with a uniform size distribution. Further TEM observation shown in Fig. 1 a and c indicates that T001 exhibits a sheet-like morphology, while T101 consists of octahedral bipyramids. The interplannar spacing of 0.35 nm shown in Fig. 1b corresponds to the typical {101} facets of anatase TiO2 [24,25]. In Fig. 1d, the lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.35 nm and the apex angle of 43.5° well evidence that T101 is enclosed by eight {101} facets.

The morphology of treated TiO2 catalysts was also investigated by HRTEM. Based on the unchanged lattice spacing of T001 and T101, pyrolysis of DCDA molecules exhibits ignorable influence on the dominant structure of TiO2, which is consistent with the XRD results. However, distinct change of surface morphology is observed for T001-DCDA and T101-DCDA. In Fig. 2 a, abundant nanodots with a small size of 2 nm are sporadically distributed on the surface of T001. Combined with the EDS analysis, these nanodots are determined to be carbon dots (CDs), meaning the formation of CDs modified TiO2 nanosheets. In contrast, amorphous outer layer, instead of surface deposits, is generally observed on the treated T101. According to our previous studies, this amorphous layer can be ascribed to the formation of oxygen-deficient TiO2, due to the reductive effect of ammonia released by pyrolyzed precursors [20]. These results indicate that exposed facets of TiO2 indeed exhibit impact on the pathways of carbohydrate pyrolysis, i.e. the facet-dependent catalysis for the pyrolysis of DCDA.

To reveal the facet-dependent influence of DCDA pyrolysis on the microstructure of TiO2, Raman spectra of different photocatalysts were collected. As shown in Fig. 3 a, five Raman peaks corresponding to the Eg(1), Eg(2), B1g(1), A1g+B1g(2), and Eg(3) modes of anatase TiO2 can be detected for all samples [26]. Besides, different from pristine T001, two characteristic peaks of graphite-like carbon are observed in the Raman spectrum of T001-DCDA. The G-band centered at 1600 cm−1 suggests the presence of sp2 carbon, while the D-band at 1400 cm−1 is attributed to the defects in the graphitic structure [27,28]. This result further confirms the formation of CDs on the surface of 001-faceted TiO2. With respect to the 101-faceted sample, in-situ pyrolysis of DCDA leads to the decreased peak intensity and broadened peak width in T101-DCDA. According to the previous reports, this can be ascribed to the lattice disorder caused by oxygen vacancies [29]. As no carbon-related signal is detected, the catalyzed pyrolysis of nitrogen-containing precursors over T101 should be favorable for the formation of non-stoichiometric TiO2. The results of N2 adsorption-desorption experiments of samples indicate all samples exhibit the IV type of nitrogen isothermal stripping curves. Meanwhile, there are slightly change in surface area after the treatment with DCDA, from 18.6 to 24.2 m2 g−1 (Figure S3) and 75.6 to 64.2 m2 g−1 (Figure S4), respectively, for T001 and T101.

ESR was employed to study the electronic structure of faceted photocatalysts. As shown in Figure S5, a weak ESR peak centered at 1.98 is observed for pristine T001, indicating the presence of small amount of Ti3+ defects. The type of defects in T101 is slightly different, as only the ESR signal caused by oxygen vacancies (g = 2.002) is detected [30]. The effect of thermal treatment on the electronic structure of TiO2 is highly dependent on its exposed facets, as shown in Fig. 4 a. The decomposition of DCDA results in the remarkable increase of the peak intensity of oxygen vacancies in T101, but possesses ignorable impact on the signal of Ti3+ in T001. This further evidences the formation of defect-induced amorphous/crystalline structures in T101-DCDA.

The chemical environment of component elements in different samples was investigated by XPS. Survey spectra of T001-DCDA and T101-DCDA shown in Figure S6 confirm that both of the samples are composed of Ti, O and C elements. Either hybridized carbon or supporting substrate can contribute to the C signal. Figure S7 shows that no obvious signal corresponding to N is detected in the samples, indicating the complete decomposition of nitrogen-containing groups. In the spectra of valence band XPS shown in Figure S8 and 4b, the VB maximum of T001 and T101 appears at 2.76 and 2.39 eV, respectively. It indicates the slight difference in the band structure of 001- and 101-faceted TiO2. Furthermore, no obvious shift of band-edge is observed between T001 and T001-DCDA. In contrast, pyrolysis of DCDA induced the right-shift of VB edge, suggesting the influence of defects on the electronic structure of T101. To gain more information, high-resolution XPS spectra are thereafter analyzed. As can be seen from Fig. 4c, there is no shift of Ti 2p2/3 and Ti 2p5/2 peaks before and after the treatment of T001. Differently, these peaks shift toward lower binding energies for T101-DCDA (Fig. 4d), indicating the formation of surface Ti3+ caused by vacancy-induced electrons [31]. O 1s spectra of T001-DCDA and T101-DCDA are further compared in Figure S9. The strong peak located at the binding energy of 529.4 eV corresponds to the lattice oxygen in TiO2. Pyrolysis of DCDA presents remarkable impact on the binding environment of oxygen atoms in T101-DCDA. Based on Gaussian convolution, the core-level spectrum exhibits three peaks, which can be ascribed to the lattice oxygen (529.4 eV), oxygen atoms near oxygen vacancy (531.8 eV) and adsorbed oxygen (533.2 eV), respectively [32]. The significant increased intensities of defected oxygen and adsorbed oxygen well evidence the activated surface sites caused by oxygen vacancies. As reported, these defect sites can facilitate the interfacial separation of charge carriers in TiO2.

Based on the above characterizations, the facet-specific formation mechanism of carbon-mediated TiO2 nanostructures was illustrated in Scheme 1 . Different from the conventional condition used to synthesize g-C3N4 (covered crucible in static air), the pyrolysis of DCDA was carried out in open air with continuous oxygen supply. In this case, the synergistic effect between direct pyrolysis and oxygen-involved oxidation facilitated the deep pyrolysis of nitrogen-containing DCDA molecules [33]. According to the previous results, exposed facets of metal oxides could not only exhibit impact on the adsorption of reactants, but also endow unique catalytic behavior for redox reactions [34–36]. Specifically, poor adsorption ability of T101 was favorable for the diffusion of pyrolysis products, resulting in the fully pyrolysis of DCDA into small molecules, even at a high concentration (30%) [37,38]. Meanwhile, the “oxygen extraction effect” caused by nitrogen-containing precursor contributed to the formation of defective T101 nanostructures [39,40]. Differently, the surface density of undercoordination Ti4+ sites in 001-faceted TiO2 was 50 % higher than that of 101-faceted TiO2. The possible catalytic effect of T001 facilitated the carbonization of strongly-adsorbed intermediates, i.e. the deposition of carbon dots onto T001 nanosheets [41].

The impact of facet-dependent pyrolysis on the photocatalytic performance of TiO2-based photocatalysts was evaluated. When different amount of DCDA is used for pyrolysis reaction, treated samples always show superior photoactivity than pristine TiO2. In the optimal condition (Fig. 5 a), the hydrogen evolution rate of T001-5% DCDA is increased from 6 to 13 mmol g−1 h−1. Thermogravimetric (TG) curves of T001-5% DCDA were recorded to calculate the weight ratio of carbon dots in the composites (Figure S10). When the temperature is increased from room temperature to 120 °C, a weight decrease of 0.5 % is noticed due to the elimination of a small amount of adsorbed water, and the weight loss of 0.42 % appearing at 100−500 °C corresponds to the combustion of carbon dots. Therefore the actual loading in T001-5% DCDA was identified as 0.42 %. This promoting effect is even more significant for 101-faceted samples, as nearly an order-of-magnitude improvement is achieved for T101-30% DCDA (Fig. 5b). It suggests that catalytic pyrolysis of organic precursors can indeed improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2. Moreover, the creation of oxygen vacancies in T101-based nanostructures seems to be more effective than the hybridization of T001 with CDs. Note that DCDA is responsible for the generation of reductive NH3, the concentration of oxygen vacancies in T101-DCDA can be well controlled by changing the mass ratio of DCDA/TiO2 (Figure S11). Photocatalytic performance of different types of TiO2 is compared in Fig. 5c. Compared to T001-5% DCDA and P25-30% DCDA, the 3 and 2 times higher hydrogen yield of T101-30% DCDA well confirms the importance of facet engineering. Furthermore, photoactivity of T101-30% DCDA is also much better than defective Vo-T101 fabricated by the conventional hydrogen reduction method. This demonstrates the fundamental contribution of pyrolysis-induced oxygen vacancies to the photocatalytic property. Besides, the cycle experiments of T101-30% DCDA show ignorable decrease in the hydrogen evolution yield in three consecutive cycles (Fig. 5d), indicating the good stability of carbon-mediated TiO2-based photocatalysts.

To further understand the underlying mechanism of the facet-dependent enhancement of photoactivity, photochemical behavior of TiO2-based nanostructures was investigated. According to the UV-vis spectra (Figure S12a and S12b), both T001 and T101 exhibits strong light absorption in the ultraviolet region. Pyrolysis of DCDA contributes to the tail absorption of TiO2 above 400 nm, especially for T001-DCDA. Based on the Tauc plots in Figure S12c and S12d, the band gap of T001 and T101 are determined to be 3.06 and 2.95 eV, respectively. A slight decrease of band gap is observed for T001-DCDA, while the pyrolysis exhibits negligible influence on that of T101.

For both T001 and T101, excitation at 325 nm give rise to broad fluorescence band centered at 390 and 475 nm, respectively (Figure S13). According to previous reports, the first peak is ascribed to band-edge transition and the latter one is associated with the defect states forming at the surface of TiO2 [42]. Pyrolysis of DCDA suppresses the emission intensity of both T001 and T101, suggesting the improved charge separation in treated nanostructures. Especially, the remarkably decreased bandgap emission of T101-DCDA proves the facilitated charge separation by surface oxygen vacancies. The charge recombination dynamics was studied by time-resoled PL spectra monitored at these emission peaks. For both emission bands, in-situ pyrolysis of DCDA over T001 results in the decrease of average fluorescence lifetimes (Fig. 6 a and b). This is not difficult to understand that CDs with high conductivity can effective extract electrons from the valence band of TiO2 (Figure S14a). [43] While, the surface treatment of T101 results in the significantly prolonged fluorescence lifetime of bandgap emission (Fig. 6c) and slightly increased lifetime of defect emission (Fig. 6d). This implies that oxygen vacancies as surface trap sites can effectively capture photo-induced electrons (Figure S14b) [44]. In Fig. 7 a, the increased photovoltaic response of defective T101-DCDA further evidences the inhibited recombination of charge carriers. The influence of impurity ions on the photoactivity and pyrolysis pathway were investigated and excluded in the supporting information. (Figure S15∼S18).

The improved interfacial behavior of charge carriers in T101-DCDA was finally studied by electrochemical measurements. Calculated from the x intercept in Mott-Schottky plots (Fig. 7b), flat band potential (Efb) of T101 at semiconductor/electrolyte interface is around -0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl. For T101-DCDA, this value is determined to be -0.53 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The positive shift of Efb is consistent with the fact that abundant oxygen vacancies are formed in T101-DCDA [45]. Benefitted from these defect levels, obviously decreased slope of Mott-Schottky plot and decreased arc radium of Nyquist plots (Fig. 7c) are achieved. This indicates that pyrolysis-induced defects contribute to the increase of donor density and the decrease of charge transfer resistance. Overall, the enhanced charge separation together with the facilitated interfacial charge transfer (Fig. 7d) result in the improved photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical performance of T101-DCDA.

In summary, we demonstrated a facile approach for improving the photoactivity of TiO2 through catalytic pyrolysis of DCDA. The differences in microstructure of faceted TiO2 led to a facet-dependent mechanism for the decomposition of DCDA molecules. Experimental investigation demonstrated that high surface energy of T001 contributed to the deposition of carbon dots onto TiO2 nanosheets, while 101 facets were favorable for the generation of oxygen vacancy defects in TiO2. The activity of facet-specific nanocatalysts for photocatalytic water splitting was evaluated, and the underlying mechanism was fundamentally investigated. This study demonstrated a facile way for developing efficient TiO2-based photocatalysts through facet engineering.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51978372, 51538013) and National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2016YFC0400502).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcatb.2019.118527.

XRD patterns of T001, T101, T001-DCDA and T101-DCDA, SEM images of T001 and T101, ESR spectra of T001 and T101, XPS survey spectra of T001-DCDA and T101-DCDA, XPS-VB spectra of T001 and T001-DCDA, XPS O 1s spectra of T001-DCDA and T101-DCDA, TEM images of T101-DCDA with different amount of DCDA, DRS spectra of T001, T001-DCDA, T101 and T101-DCDA, Tauc plots of T001, T001-DCDA, T101 and T101-DCDA, PL spectra of T001, T001-DCDA, T101 and T101-DCDA, Schematic diagrams of interfacial charge transfer in T001-DCDA and T101-DCDA.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Titanium dioxide nanomaterials for photovoltaic applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Bai, I. Mora-Seró, F. De Angelis, J. Bisquert, P. Wang, Titanium dioxide nanomaterials for photovoltaic applications, Chem. Rev. 114 (2014) 10095-10130.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Titanium dioxide-based nanomaterials for photocatalytic fuel generations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ma, X. Wang, Y. Jia, X. Chen, H. Han, C. Li, Titanium dioxide-based nanomaterials for photocatalytic fuel generations, Chem. Rev. 114 (2014) 9987-10043.
L. Liu, X. Chen, Titanium dioxide nanomaterials: Self-structural modifications, Chem. Rev. 114 (2014) 9890-9918.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Noble metals-TiO2 nanocomposites: from fundamental mechanisms to photocatalysis, surface enhanced Raman scattering and antibacterial applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Prakash, S. Sun, H.C. Swart, R.K. Gupta, Noble metals-TiO2 nanocomposites: From fundamental mechanisms to photocatalysis, surface enhanced Raman scattering and antibacterial applications, Appl. Mater. Today. 11 (2018) 82-135.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface localization of defects in black TiO2: enhancing photoactivity or reactivity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Zhang, J.H. Park, Surface localization of defects in black TiO2: Enhancing photoactivity or reactivity, J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 8 (2017) 199-207.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis, properties, and applications of black titanium dioxide nanomaterials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, L. Tian, X. Tan, X. Li, X. Chen, Synthesis, properties, and applications of black titanium dioxide nanomaterials, Sci. Bull. 62 (2017) 431-441.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structural evolution of titanium dioxide during reduction in high-pressure hydrogen

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Selcuk, X. Zhao, A. Selloni, Structural evolution of titanium dioxide during reduction in high-pressure hydrogen, Nat. Mater. 17 (2018) 923-928.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Black TiO2 nanomaterials: a review of recent advances

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.G. Ullattil, S.B. Narendranath, S.C. Pillai, P. Periyat, Black TiO2 nanomaterials: A review of recent advances, Chem. Eng. J. 343 (2018) 708-736.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on the visible light active titanium dioxide photocatalysts for environmental applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Pelaez, N.T. Nolan, S.C. Pillai, M.K. Seery, P. Falaras, A.G. Kontos, P.S.M. Dunlop, J.W.J. Hamilton, J.A. Byrne, K. O’Shea, M.H. Entezari, D.D. Dionysiou, A review on the visible light active titanium dioxide photocatalysts for environmental applications, Appl. Catal. B: Environ. 125 (2012) 331-349.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface modification and enhanced photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance of TiO2: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Low, B. Cheng, J. Yu, Surface modification and enhanced photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance of TiO2: a review, Appl. Surf. Sci. 392 (2017) 658-686.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Passivation of defect states in anatate TiO2 hollow spheres with Mg doping: realizing efficient photocatalytic overall water splitting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Gao, Y. Li, J. Ren, S. Wang, R. Wang, G. Fu, Y. Hu, Passivation of defect states in anatate TiO2 hollow spheres with Mg doping: Realizing efficient photocatalytic overall water splitting, Appl. Catal. B: Environ. 202 (2017) 127-133.
